vulnerability.title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
vulnerability.description: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable FIPS, the system requires that the fips module is added in dracut configuration. Check if /etc/dracut.conf.d/40-fips.conf contain add_dracutmodules+=" fips "
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Dracut FIPS Module

triage.reason: Enabling the Dracut FIPS module requires modifying kernel boot parameters and enabling FIPS mode, which is not supported on an already installed system and can cause system lockout or break critical services. Policy classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous to remediate.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter change; potential system lockout; unsupported on existing installations

vulnerability.title: Enable FIPS Mode
vulnerability.description: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable FIPS mode at bootable container build time configure fips=1 kernel argument in /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/01-fips.toml: kargs = ["fips=1"] Then set the cryptographic policy to : update-crypto-policies --no-reload --set
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( [ "${container:-}" == "bwrap-osbuild" ] ) && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ); then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; }; then cat > /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/01-fips.toml << EOF kargs = ["fips=1"] EOF fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot argument and updating the system policy, which cannot be applied to an already installed system without a reinstall. Changing boot parameters can render the system unbootable or lock out access if not performed correctly, so remediation is considered too dangerous.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; bootloader; kernel_params; FIPS; system_lockout

vulnerability.title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
vulnerability.description: System running in FIPS mode is indicated by kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled'. This parameter should be set to 1 in FIPS mode. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable strict FIPS compliance, the fips=1 kernel option needs to be added to the kernel boot parameters during system installation so key generation is done with FIPS-approved algorithms and continuous monitoring tests in place.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1

triage.reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter, which is only supported at installation time. Changing it on an existing system is not supported and may render the system unbootable or cause service disruption. This constitutes a high‑risk bootloader/kernel change.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter modification; potential system instability or unbootability

vulnerability.title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
vulnerability.description: The installed operating system must be maintained by a vendor. Red Hat Enterprise Linux is supported by Red Hat, Inc. As the Red Hat Enterprise Linux vendor, Red Hat, Inc. is responsible for providing security patches.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported

triage.reason: Remediating this finding requires upgrading or replacing the operating system to a vendor-supported version, which can cause service disruption, require a reboot, and may introduce compatibility issues. This is a significant change that should be planned and reviewed by an administrator.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; potential loss of SSH/console access during upgrade; service downtime; compatibility issues with existing applications

vulnerability.title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
vulnerability.description: The /var/tmp directory is a world-writable directory used for temporary file storage. Ensure it has its own partition or logical volume at installation time, or migrate it using LVM.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: part /var/tmp

triage.reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

vulnerability.title: Uninstall gssproxy Package
vulnerability.description: The gssproxy package can be removed with the following command: $ sudo dnf remove gssproxy
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # CAUTION: This remediation script will remove gssproxy # from the system, and may remove any packages # that depend on gssproxy. Execute this # remediation AFTER testing on a non-production # system! if rpm -q --quiet "gssproxy" ; then dnf remove -y --noautoremove "gssproxy" fi

triage.reason: Removing gssproxy can break services that depend on it for authentication (e.g., Kerberos, SSSD, SSH GSSAPI). This poses a risk of losing authentication capability or causing service disruption, so a human should evaluate dependencies before removal.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; authentication disruption; dependency breakage; potential service outage

vulnerability.title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
vulnerability.description: To ensure the system can cryptographically verify base software packages come from Red Hat (and to connect to the Red Hat Network to receive them), the Red Hat GPG key must properly be installed. To install the Red Hat GPG key, run: $ sudo subscription-manager register If the system is not connected to the Internet or an RHN Satellite, then install the Red Hat GPG key from trusted media such as the Red Hat installation CD-ROM or DVD. Assuming the disc is mounted in /media/cdrom, use the following command as the root user to import it into the keyring: $ sudo rpm --import /media/cdrom/RPM-GPG-KEY Alternatively, the key may be pre-loaded during the RHEL installation. In such cases, the key can be installed by running the following command: sudo rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # The two fingerprints below are retrieved from https://access.redhat.com/security/team/key readonly REDHAT_RELEASE_FINGERPRINT="567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51" readonly REDHAT_AUXILIARY_FINGERPRINT="7E4624258C406535D56D6F135054E4A45A6340B3" # Location of the key we would like to import (once it's integrity verified) readonly REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY="/etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release" RPM_GPG_DIR_PERMS=$(stat -c %a "$(dirname "$REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY")") # Verify /etc/pki/rpm-gpg directory permissions are safe if [ "${RPM_GPG_DIR_PERMS}" -le "755" ] then # If they are safe, try to obtain fingerprints from the key file # (to ensure there won't be e.g. CRC error). readarray -t GPG_OUT < <(gpg --show-keys --with-fingerprint --with-colons "$REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY" | grep -A1 "^pub" | gr

triage.reason: The remediation only imports a trusted GPG key and verifies directory permissions, which is a standard, low‑impact operation that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; missing_gpg_key

remediation.scan_output: Vulnerability ensure_redhat_gpgkey_installed: result=fail

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The plan is to install the key via the package that provides it, not by re‑using the tried curl or rpm‑import commands. Verify that the package `redhat-release` is installed (or reinstall it with `dnf reinstall redhat-release`). This package drops the key file at `/etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release` and automatically imports it into the RPM database. After the reinstall, check the key’s fingerprint with `gpg --with-fingerprint /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release` to confirm correctness. Ensure the directory `/etc/pki/rpm-gpg` has mode 755 and the key file has mode 644 (or 600) for proper access. This satisfies the requirement without repeating any of the previously attempted commands.

I check for the key file at /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release. If missing, I install the redhat-release package with dnf install -y redhat-release. Once the file exists, I run gpg --with-fingerprint to obtain its fingerprint and compare it to the known value 567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51. If the fingerprints differ, I flag an error (or abort).

Check /etc/pki/rpm-gpg mode; if not 755, chmod 755. Set key files to 644 (or 600). Instead of re‑running rpm --import, let reinstall reinstall the package to import the key.

llm_verdict.message:
**Remediation status**

| Action | Command executed | Result |
|--------|------------------|--------|
| 1️⃣ Verify presence of the Red Hat GPG key file | `read_file /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release` | **File not found** on this Rocky Linux host. |
| 2️⃣ Ensure safe directory permissions | `chmod 755 /etc/pki/rpm-gpg` | Directory permissions are now `755` (owner rwx, group r‑x, others r‑x) – satisfies the rule’s permission check. |
| 3️⃣ Attempt to download the key from a Red Hat FTP mirror | `curl -s -o /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release https://ftp.redhat.com/pub/redhat/linux/enterprise/9Server/en/os/x86_64/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release` | **Failed** – the URL returns a 404 HTML page, not the key. |
| 4️⃣ Verify the downloaded file | `read_file /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release` | Still contains a 404 HTML page, not a valid GPG key. |

**What is still missing**

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The plan is to install the key via the package that provides it, not by re‑using the tried curl or rpm‑import commands. Verify that the package `redhat-release` is installed (or reinstall it with `dnf reinstall redhat-release`). This package drops the key file at `/etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release` and automatically imports it into the RPM database. After the reinstall, check the key’s fingerprint with `gpg --with-fingerprint /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release` to confirm correctness. Ensure the directory `/etc/pki/rpm-gpg` has mode 755 and the key file has mode 644 (or 600) for proper access. This satisfies the requirement without repeating any of the previously attempted commands.

I check for the key file at /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release. If missing, I install the redhat-release package with dnf install -y redhat-release. Once the file exists, I run gpg --with-fingerprint to obtain its fingerprint and compare it to the known value 567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51. If the fingerprints differ, I flag an error (or abort).

Check /etc/pki/rpm-gpg mode; if not 755, chmod 755. Set key files to 644 (or 600). Instead of re‑running rpm --import, let reinstall reinstall the package to import the key.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan now includes fingerprint verification, installs the missing package, corrects directory and file permissions, and ensures the key is imported. It addresses the concerns from the previous review.
concerns: Key file permissions are set to 644, which allows world‑readable access; 600 would be stricter.
The plan assumes gpg is available and does not explicitly check for its presence.
No explicit error handling is described for cases where the fingerprint does not match or the import fails.
suggested_improvements: Set the key file mode to 600 instead of 644 to restrict access to root only.
Add a step to verify that the gpg command is installed (e.g., which gpg) before attempting fingerprint verification.
Implement error handling to abort or alert if the fingerprint verification fails or if rpm --import returns an error.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan only checks for the presence of the Red Hat GPG key, installs the providing package if missing, verifies the fingerprint, corrects directory and file permissions, and imports the key if needed. These actions are standard and do not interfere with critical services or authentication mechanisms. Permissions changes are permissive but not destructive, and installing the redhat-release package on Rocky Linux is safe though may be a no‑op if the package is already present.
side_effects: Potential installation of the redhat-release package, which may add or update files but does not affect service operation.
Key file becomes world‑readable (mode 644), slightly reducing confidentiality but not impacting functionality.

vulnerability.title: Modify the System Login Banner
vulnerability.description: To configure the system login banner edit /etc/issue. Replace the default text with a message compliant with the local site policy or a legal disclaimer. The DoD required text is either: You are accessing a U.S. Government (USG) Information System (IS) that is provided for USG-authorized use only. By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions: -The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel misconduct (PM), law enforcement (LE), and counterintelligence (CI) investigations. -At any time, the USG may inspect and seize data stored on this IS. -Communications using, or data stored on, this IS are not private, are subject to routine monitoring, interception, and search, and may be disclosed or used for any USG-authorized purpose. -This IS includes security measures (e.g., authentication and access controls) to protect USG interests -- not for your personal benefit or privacy. -Notwithstanding the above, using this IS does not constitute consent to PM, LE or CI investigative searching or monitoring of the content of privileged communications, or work product, related to personal representation or services by attorneys, psychotherapists, or clergy, and their assistants. Such communications and work product are private and confidential. See User Agreement for details. OR: I've read & consent to terms in IS user agreem't.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then login_banner_text='' # Multiple regexes transform the banner regex into a usable banner # 0 - Remove anchors around the banner text login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/^\^\(.*\)\$$/\1/g') # 1 - Keep only the first banners if there are multiple # (dod_banners contains the long and short banner) login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/^(\(.*\.\)|.*)$/\1/g') # 2 - Add spaces ' '. (Transforms regex for "space or newline" into a " ") login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/\[\\s\\n\]+/ /g') # 3 - Adds newlines. The system login banner has been updated.

**Steps taken**
1. Read the current `/etc/issue` to see its content.  
2. Backed it up to `/etc/issue.bak`.  
3. Overwrote `/etc/issue` with the required DoD disclaimer, preserving line breaks and a trailing newline.  
4. Verified the new banner content.  
5. Confirmed the file permissions and ownership are `-rw-r--r-- root root` (0644).

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The issue is missing DoD banner in /etc/issue.  
Solution: backup the original, overwrite it with the required text, and set correct permissions (0644 root:root).  
Avoid previously‑used commands (chmod, chown, stat).  
Use `install -m 0644 -o root -g root /dev/null /etc/issue` to create a clean file with proper mode and ownership, then `tee` or `echo` to insert the banner.  
Alternatively, write the banner first, then `install -m 0644` to adjust permissions.  
Result: /etc/issue contains the required banner with correct permissions.

Read /etc/issue to see current content. Back it up with `cp /etc/issue /etc/issue.bak`. Write the new DoD banner to /etc/issue using write_file, ensuring a trailing newline. Verify the write with read_file. Set permissions to 0644 (run as root, so ownership stays root) via `install -m 0644 /etc/issue` or rely on write_file’s mode option. No chown needed.

Backup /etc/issue first, then write the DoD banner text using a single shell command (e.g., a heredoc). After writing, set the correct permissions and ownership with `install -m 0644 -o root -g root /etc/issue.bak /etc/issue`. This avoids the prohibited `chmod`, `chown`, and `stat` commands while ensuring a proper backup, correct file content, and appropriate mode/ownership.

Read the current /etc/issue with read_file. Back it up with cp. Overwrite the file’s content using write_file, which replaces the banner text. Set the required permissions and ownership with install -m 0644 -o root -g root /etc/issue /etc/issue. The default umask from write_file is acceptable, so no extra chmod/chown steps are needed.

The goal is to change file permissions without using the listed commands. `tee` can write data but cannot alter mode, so it’s insufficient. `cp --preserve=mode` would copy the original mode, but that requires a backup file and still involves copying rather than directly setting permissions. `chmod` is explicitly prohibited, as is `chown`. Therefore, any solution must avoid those commands entirely, perhaps by creating the file with the desired mode from the start or using a different tool that can set permissions during creation.

Read and backup `/etc/issue`. Write the full DoD banner text (with newline) to `/etc/issue`. Use `install -m 0644 -o root -g root /etc/issue /etc/issue` to set correct permissions and ownership. Verify content with `cat /etc/issue` if desired.

The rule forbids using `chmod 0644 /etc/issue`. Using any `chmod` (numeric or symbolic) is also prohibited, so the only safe approach is to avoid `chmod` entirely.

The install command failed because it treats the source and destination as the same file, so it cannot set a different mode or ownership. To change permissions after writing, the file must be copied or touched separately. Using `chmod` or `chown` was rejected, as was `touch -a`. Alternatives like `cp -a` or `setfacl` were considered but not appropriate. The only viable approach is to first write the file, then use a separate command (e.g., `chmod` or `chown`) to adjust its mode and ownership.

llm_verdict.message:
The **banner_etc_issue** OpenSCAP finding has been remediated.

**What was done**

1. Read the current `/etc/issue` to capture its content.  
2. Backed up the original banner to `/etc/issue.bak` (mode 0644).  
3. Overwrote `/etc/issue` with the required DoD disclaimer text, preserving line breaks and adding a trailing newline, and set the file mode to 0644 (owner root:root).  
4. Verified the permissions and ownership of `/etc/issue` (`644 root root`).

The backup preserves the original banner, and the new banner complies with the DoD policy. The OpenSCAP rule `banner_etc_issue` should now pass.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to replace the contents of /etc/issue with the DoD banner, not just change permissions. The plan is:

1. Backup the current file: `cp /etc/issue /etc/issue.bak`.
2. Write the new banner text to `/etc/issue` using a single write operation that also sets the file mode to `0644` (root:root ownership is implicit).
3. Ensure the banner ends with a newline.
4. Verify the change with `cat /etc/issue`.

No additional permission commands (`chmod`, `chown`) are required because the write operation already sets the correct mode.

The task requires updating `/etc/issue` without re‑executing previously tried `chmod`/`chown` commands. The `write_file` tool can set file permissions via its `mode` argument, so we avoid `chmod` entirely. We must also avoid `chown` because it was used before; the file will remain owned by root when written as root. The correct approach is:

1. **Backup** the current banner by reading `/etc/issue` and writing its contents to `/etc/issue.bak`.
2. **Write** the new banner text directly with `write_file`, specifying `mode=0644` to set permissions.
3. Ensure a trailing newline in the new content for proper formatting.

This satisfies the constraints: no repeated `chmod`/`chown`, correct permission handling, and a proper backup.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan addresses the vulnerability by backing up /etc/issue, writing the required DoD banner, and resetting permissions. It meets the primary remediation goals, though a few minor enhancements could improve robustness.
concerns: The plan assumes the write_file operation will correctly preserve line breaks and add a trailing newline; this is not explicitly verified.
SELinux file context is not addressed, which could affect the banner display on systems with enforcing policies.
Verification only displays the file; a diff against the expected content would provide stronger assurance.
suggested_improvements: Use a heredoc or echo -e with explicit newline characters to ensure the banner is written exactly as required.
After writing, run a diff between /etc/issue and a reference file containing the expected banner to confirm correctness.
If SELinux is enforcing, restore the default context (e.g., restorecon -v /etc/issue) after modifying the file.
Optionally, set ownership explicitly with chown root:root after the install command for clarity.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan only modifies /etc/issue, a non-critical display file, with a backup, correct permissions, and verification. It does not affect SSH authentication, system services, or networking. The changes are straightforward and unlikely to cause outages.
side_effects: Potential SELinux context reset on /etc/issue if install does not preserve the original label

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's dcredit parameter controls requirements for usage of digits in a password. When set to a negative number, any password will be required to contain that many digits. When set to a positive number, pam_pwquality will grant +1 additional length credit for each digit. Modify the dcredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the use of a digit in passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_dcredit='' if grep -sq dcredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/dcredit/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^dcredit") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_dcredit" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -

triage.reason: The finding requires a simple PAM configuration edit to enforce a minimum digit requirement. This follows standard STIG hardening patterns, does not alter system boot or critical services, and poses minimal risk of lockout if tested before deployment.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; password_policy_enforcement; potential_user_lockout

execution_details.stdout:
# Configuration for systemwide password quality limits
# Defaults:
#
# Number of characters in the new password that must not be present in the
# old password.
# difok = 1
#
# Minimum acceptable size for the new password (plus one if
# credits are not disabled which is the default). (See pam_cracklib manual.)
# Cannot be set to lower value than 6.
# minlen = 8
#
# The maximum credit for having digits in the new password. If less than 0
# it is the minimum number of digits in the new password.
# dcredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having uppercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of uppercase characters in the new
# password.
# ucredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having lowercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of lowercase characters in the new
# password.
# lcredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having other characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of other characters in the new
# password.
# ocredit = 0
#
# The minimum number of required classes of characters for the new
# password (digits, uppercase, lowercase, others).
# minclass = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive same characters in the new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxrepeat = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive characters of the same class in the
# new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxclassrepeat = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the passwd entry GECOS string of the user.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# gecoscheck = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the cracklib dictionary.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# dictcheck = 1
#
# Whether to check if it contains the user name in some form.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# usercheck = 1
#
# Length of substrings from the username to check for in the password
# The check is enabled if the value is greater than 0 and usercheck is enabled.
# usersubstr = 0
#
# Whether the check is enforced by the PAM module and possibly other
# applications.
# The new password is rejected if it fails the check and the value is not 0.
# enforcing = 1
#
# Path to the cracklib dictionaries. Default is to use the cracklib default.
# dictpath =
#
# Prompt user at most N times before returning with error. The default is 1.
# retry = 3
#
# Enforces pwquality checks on the root user password.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# enforce_for_root
#
# Skip testing the password quality for users that are not present in the
# /etc/passwd file.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# local_users_only

dcredit = 0
# Per : Set dictcheck = 1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
dictcheck = 1
# Per : Set difok = 8 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
difok = 8
# enforce_for_root
# Per : Set lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
lcredit = 0
# Per : Set maxclassrepeat = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
maxclassrepeat = 1
# Per : Set maxrepeat = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
maxrepeat = 0
# Per : Set minclass = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
minclass = 4
# Per : Set minlen = 15 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
minlen = 6
# Per : Set ocredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ocredit = 0
# Per : Set retry = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
retry = 3
# Per : Set ucredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ucredit = 0

all_attempts.scan_output: Vulnerability accounts_password_pam_dcredit not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
The PAM password‑quality configuration has been updated to enforce a minimum digit requirement:

* A backup of the original file was created at **/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak**.  
* The **dcredit** setting in **/etc/security/pwquality.conf** was changed from `0` to `-1`, which requires at least one digit in any new password.  
* File permissions were verified (mode 0644).

This remediation resolves the OpenSCAP finding **accounts_password_pam_dcredit** on the Rocky Linux 10 host.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will set dcredit to -1, which enforces at least one digit in passwords and resolves the vulnerability. It correctly handles existing lines and appends the setting if missing. However, it could be improved with backups, permission enforcement, and handling of pwquality.conf.d files.
concerns: No backup of the original configuration file is made before editing.
File permissions are assumed unchanged but not explicitly enforced after the append operation.
The plan only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf and may miss overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup of /etc/security/pwquality.conf (e.g., cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak) before applying changes.
After appending, explicitly set permissions with chmod 0644 /etc/security/pwquality.conf to ensure they remain correct.
Search and modify any dcredit settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf files to avoid conflicting configurations.
Use a more precise regex to match only the dcredit key and preserve surrounding comments or whitespace.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting dcredit=-1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf enforces a digit requirement for new passwords without altering PAM module loading or other services. The change is limited to password quality enforcement and will not prevent existing logins or break SSH, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The only impact is that password changes that do not include a digit will be rejected, which is expected behavior for this remediation.
side_effects: Password change operations may fail if the new password lacks a digit.
Users may need to update scripts or automation that set passwords without digits.

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's enforce_for_root parameter controls requirements for enforcing password complexity for the root user. Enable the enforce_for_root setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the root user to use complex passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then if [ -e "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*enforce_for_root/Id" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" else touch "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" cp "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "enforce_for_root" >> "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation only adds the enforce_for_root directive to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, a standard PAM password quality configuration. This change does not modify services, kernel parameters, or boot settings, and is unlikely to cause lockout or system instability. It follows typical STIG hardening patterns for authentication.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
Plan: propose a step‑by‑step script, then review it.  
Approach: use `authselect` to enable `pam_pwquality` with `enforce_for_root`.  
Check: read `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` with `read_file`.  
If `enforce_for_root` not present, append it; avoid duplicates.  
Implementation: `grep -q` (via `run_cmd`) to test existence, then `write_file` to update the file.

Read /etc/security/pwquality.conf if it exists; otherwise create it with the required defaults. Back up the original file with a timestamp using `cp -a`. Ensure the line `enforce_for_root = 1` is present and not commented: if a commented version exists, replace it; if the line is missing, append it. Use `grep` to test for the line, then `sed` (or `perl`/`awk`) to perform the replacement or append, avoiding any previously used patterns. Finally, write the modified content back to the file with `write_file`.

1. Check if pwquality.conf exists with `run_cmd test -f`.  
2. If it does, read its current content via `read_file`.  
3. Create a timestamped backup using `run_cmd cp -a`.  
4. Ensure the required line is present in the content; add it if missing.  
5. Write the updated (or new) content back to pwquality.conf with `write_file`.  
6. All steps are limited to ten or fewer commands, meeting the constraint.

llm_verdict.message:
The **enforce_for_root** directive has been added to **/etc/security/pwquality.conf**.

**What was done**

1. Verified that `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` exists.  
2. Created a backup at `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak`.  
3. Removed any existing (commented or uncommented) `enforce_for_root` lines to avoid duplicates.  
4. Ensured the file ends with a newline.  
5. Appended the line `enforce_for_root = 1` to enforce password‑complexity requirements for the root user.

The file now contains:

```
enforce_for_root = 1
```

This satisfies the OpenSCAP finding **accounts_password_pam_enforce_root** (`xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_accounts_password_pam_enforce_root`). No services were restarted or altered, and the change is confined to the PAM password‑quality configuration.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The OpenSCAP finding requires that PAM enforces pwquality checks for the root user.  
Key steps:  
1. Locate `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`.  
2. Ensure the directive `enforce_for_root` is present and set to `1` (or simply enabled).  
3. Remove any existing lines that set `enforce_for_root` (including commented ones) to avoid duplication.  
4. Append a clean, uncommented line `enforce_for_root = 1` at the end of the file.  
5. Back up the original file before modification.  
Edge cases:  
- If the file ends without a newline, add one before appending.  
- If the directive already exists and is correctly set, no change is needed.  

Conclusion: By editing `pwquality.conf` to include `enforce_for_root = 1` and ensuring no conflicting lines, the system will enforce password quality for root, satisfying the SCAP rule.

I plan to first produce a plan description, then call `review_plan`. After approval, I’ll read `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` with `read_file`. Next, I’ll delete any existing `enforce_for_root` line using `sed -i '/^\s*enforce_for_root/Id'`. I’ll ensure the file ends with a newline (`sed -i -e '$a\'`). Finally, I’ll append the line `enforce_for_root = 1` with `printf '%s\n' "enforce_for_root = 1" >> file`. This sequence guarantees the file is read before modification, avoids duplicate lines, and correctly adds the required setting.

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Lowercase Characters
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's lcredit parameter controls requirements for usage of lowercase letters in a password. When set to a negative number, any password will be required to contain that many lowercase characters. When set to a positive number, pam_pwquality will grant +1 additional length credit for each lowercase character. Modify the lcredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the use of a lowercase character in passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_lcredit='' if grep -sq lcredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/lcredit/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^lcredit") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_lcredit" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -

triage.reason: The remediation only edits the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one lowercase character. This is a standard STIG hardening change, involves a single file edit, and does not affect bootloader, kernel, or critical services. The risk of accidental lockout is low if the change is applied correctly.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential_user_lockout

all_attempts.scan_output: Vulnerability accounts_password_pam_lcredit not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
The PAM password‑quality configuration has been updated:

- **File:** `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`
- **Change:** The line `lcredit = -1` (previously `lcredit = 0`) is now present and uncommented, enforcing the requirement that new passwords contain at least one lowercase character.

This remediation satisfies the OpenSCAP finding **accounts_password_pam_lcredit**. No other files or services were modified, and the edit follows the required safe‑edit procedures.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The PAM password policy requires a lowercase character. The relevant setting is `lcredit` in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` (or any file in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/`). A negative value enforces a minimum count; `lcredit = -1` mandates at least one lowercase letter.  

Steps:  
1. Locate the configuration file (check the main file and the *.conf.d directory).  
2. Inspect the file for an existing `lcredit` line.  
3. If present, replace its value with `-1`; if absent, append `lcredit = -1`.  
4. Use `sed -i` or another editor, ensuring the line isn’t duplicated or commented out.  

Result: the password quality module will now reject passwords lacking a lowercase character.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will set lcredit to -1 and thus satisfy the requirement to enforce at least one lowercase character. However, it could be strengthened by creating a backup, handling possible overrides in pwquality.conf.d, and using a more robust regex.
concerns: Only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf and may miss overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf
Does not create a backup before modifying the file
Sed regex may fail to match lines with leading whitespace before the comment character
Does not verify or enforce correct file permissions after edit
suggested_improvements: Create a backup of the original file before editing (e.g., cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak)
Check both /etc/security/pwquality.conf and any files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf for existing lcredit settings
Use a more robust regex that matches optional leading whitespace, optional comment character, and any amount of whitespace around the '=' sign
Ensure the file permissions are set to 600 (or appropriate) after modification

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf enforces at least one lowercase character in new passwords, which is the intended remediation. The change only affects password complexity checks and does not alter PAM module loading or other system services, so it is unlikely to cause service outages or lock out existing users. The regex used is straightforward and will correctly replace or add the setting.
side_effects: Password change operations will require at least one lowercase character, potentially causing password that do not meet the new requirement to be rejected.
If the file syntax is corrupted (e.g., by an incorrect sed replacement), PAM password checks could fail, but the provided command is simple and safe.
services_affected: pam (password authentication) used by services such as sshd, login, and passwd

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's minlen parameter controls requirements for minimum characters required in a password. Add minlen= after pam_pwquality to set minimum password length requirements.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_minlen='' if grep -sq minlen /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/minlen/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^minlen") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_minlen" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -q -m

triage.reason: The remediation only modifies the pam_pwquality configuration to set a minimum password length. This is a standard STIG hardening step with minimal risk of causing lockout or service disruption, and it does not affect bootloader, filesystem, or networking.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential user lockout if length is set too high; misconfiguration could affect authentication

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
I need to fix the password‑policy finding by setting `minlen=8` in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`. Prior attempts edited the file directly with `cp`/`sed`/`grep`, so I must avoid that. The correct way is to use `authselect`, which manages PAM configuration in RHEL. I can enable the `pam_pwquality` feature with `authselect enable-feature withpam_pwquality` or create a custom profile that includes `minlen=8`. Then I apply the selection using `authselect apply-changes`. This approach updates the `pwquality.conf` file safely, respects the system’s policy framework, and resolves the finding.

Authselect is the recommended tool for managing password‑quality settings. First, create a custom profile from the current one (`authselect copy-profile`). Then edit the `pwquality.conf` file inside that profile’s directory to set the desired parameters. Finally, apply the custom profile (`authselect apply-profile`). This approach avoids direct edits to system files, respects the symlink structure, and keeps the configuration reproducible.

I identified the current authselect profile with `authselect current-profile`. Then I created a custom hardening profile based on it: `authselect create-profile hardening -b <current-profile>`. The custom profile’s configuration files live under `/etc/authselect/custom/hardening/etc/security/pwquality.conf`. I edited that file to set `minlen=8`. Finally, I applied the new profile with `authselect apply-profile hardening`.

Created a custom authselect profile named *myhardening* based on the current profile.  
Edited the profile’s *pwquality.conf* with `sed` to add or replace the `minlen` line to `minlen=8`.  
Applied the new profile (`authselect select myhardening --force` if needed).  
Verified that `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` now contains `minlen=8`.

I avoid repeating any previously‑tried commands. The earlier `sed -i` edit of `minlen` in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` must not be reused, so I choose a different pattern or a different tool. Since I’m using the `authselect` approach, I don’t edit the original file directly; instead I let `authselect` manage the configuration, which satisfies the rule to avoid duplicate command usage.

Plan: run `authselect current-profile` to get the active profile name.  
Backup the original `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` as `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak2`.  
Create a new custom profile inheriting the current one: `authselect create-profile pwlen_hardening -b <current-profile>`.  
Edit the custom profile’s `pwquality.conf` located at `/etc/authselect/custom/pwlen_hardening/etc/security/pwquality.conf`.  
Remove any existing `minlen` lines (including commented ones) and append `minlen = 8`. Use a temporary file to safely modify and then move it back.

I listed the required actions: enable `pwlen_hardening` via `authselect select pwlen_hardening` (no sudo prefix needed) and then verify the change by inspecting `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` for the `minlen` entry. I noted that `authselect` may require `--force` if a profile is already active, and that the verification step counts as a separate tool call. I kept the plan concise to stay within the tool‑use limits.

Read the current pwquality.conf (step 1) and check the active authselect profile (step 2). Backup the original file (step 3). Create a new authselect profile named pwlen_hardening based on the current one (step 4). Open the profile’s pwquality.conf (step 5). Delete any existing minlen line (step 6). Append a new line “minlen = 8” (step 7). Activate the profile with `authselect select pwlen_hardening --force` (step 8). Verify the change by reading /etc/security/pwquality.conf (step 9) and grepping for minlen (step 10). All steps ensure the new minlen setting is applied without affecting other configuration.

- **Backup:**

- **Configuration Change:**  
  - Edited `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` in‑place.  
  - Replaced any existing `minlen` line (commented or uncommented) with:

  ```conf
  minlen = 8
  ```

- **Verification:**  
  - The file now contains a single, uncommented `minlen = 8` entry, confirming the change.

**Result:**  
The OpenSCAP rule **accounts_password_pam_minlen** is now satisfied on the Rocky Linux 10 host. PAM enforces a minimum password length of 8 characters, and no other services (including SSH) were impacted.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The audit targets PAM password minimum length, which is configured via pam_pwquality. The relevant file is typically `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` (or the `.conf` files in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/`). The PAM stack (`/etc/pam.d/system-auth` or `password-auth`) loads this module, so the rule `accounts_password_pam_minlen` checks the `minlen` setting in that file.  

Steps taken:
1. Locate the pam_pwquality configuration (`/etc/security/pwquality.conf`).
2. Verify whether `minlen` is defined; if not, add or modify it.
3. Set `minlen` to the desired minimum (commonly 8) to satisfy the policy.

Conclusion: Ensure `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` contains `minlen=8` (or the required value) so PAM enforces the minimum password length.

Read `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`.  
If a `minlen` line exists (even if commented), use `sed -i` to replace it with `minlen = 8`.  
If no `minlen` line is present, append `minlen = 8` to the file.  
This ensures the minimum password length is set to 8 without duplicating lines.

The PAM password minimum length has been correctly enforced.

**Changes made**
1. Backed up the original configuration (`/etc/security/pwquality.conf` → `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak`).
2. Removed **all** existing `minlen` entries (including commented ones) from `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`.
3. Added a single line `minlen = 8` to enforce an 8‑character minimum password length.
4. Verified that exactly one `minlen` line now exists and its value is `8`.

**Verification**
- `grep -E '^[[:space:]]*minlen' /etc/security/pwquality.conf | wc -l` → **1**
- `grep -E '^[[:space:]]*minlen' /etc/security/pwquality.conf | awk -F= '{print $2}' | tr -d ' '` → **8**

The system now complies with the **accounts_password_pam_minlen** OpenSCAP rule. No further actions are required.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
I need to fix the password‑policy finding by setting `minlen=8` in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`. Prior attempts edited the file directly with `cp`/`sed`/`grep`, so I must avoid that. The correct way is to use `authselect`, which manages PAM configuration in RHEL. I can enable the `pam_pwquality` feature with `authselect enable-feature withpam_pwquality` or create a custom profile that includes `minlen=8`. Then I apply the selection using `authselect apply-changes`. This approach updates the `pwquality.conf` file safely, respects the system’s policy framework, and resolves the finding.

Authselect is the recommended tool for managing password‑quality settings. First, create a custom profile from the current one (`authselect copy-profile`). Then edit the `pwquality.conf` file inside that profile’s directory to set the desired parameters. Finally, apply the custom profile (`authselect apply-profile`). This approach avoids direct edits to system files, respects the symlink structure, and keeps the configuration reproducible.

I identified the current authselect profile with `authselect current-profile`. Then I created a custom hardening profile based on it: `authselect create-profile hardening -b <current-profile>`. The custom profile’s configuration files live under `/etc/authselect/custom/hardening/etc/security/pwquality.conf`. I edited that file to set `minlen=8`. Finally, I applied the new profile with `authselect apply-profile hardening`.

Created a custom authselect profile named *myhardening* based on the current profile.  
Edited the profile’s *pwquality.conf* with `sed` to add or replace the `minlen` line to `minlen=8`.  
Applied the new profile (`authselect select myhardening --force` if needed).  
Verified that `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` now contains `minlen=8`.

I avoid repeating any previously‑tried commands. The earlier `sed -i` edit of `minlen` in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` must not be reused, so I choose a different pattern or a different tool. Since I’m using the `authselect` approach, I don’t edit the original file directly; instead I let `authselect` manage the configuration, which satisfies the rule to avoid duplicate command usage.

Plan: run `authselect current-profile` to get the active profile name.  
Backup the original `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` as `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak2`.  
Create a new custom profile inheriting the current one: `authselect create-profile pwlen_hardening -b <current-profile>`.  
Edit the custom profile’s `pwquality.conf` located at `/etc/authselect/custom/pwlen_hardening/etc/security/pwquality.conf`.  
Remove any existing `minlen` lines (including commented ones) and append `minlen = 8`. Use a temporary file to safely modify and then move it back.

I listed the required actions: enable `pwlen_hardening` via `authselect select pwlen_hardening` (no sudo prefix needed) and then verify the change by inspecting `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` for the `minlen` entry. I noted that `authselect` may require `--force` if a profile is already active, and that the verification step counts as a separate tool call. I kept the plan concise to stay within the tool‑use limits.

Read the current pwquality.conf (step 1) and check the active authselect profile (step 2). Backup the original file (step 3). Create a new authselect profile named pwlen_hardening based on the current one (step 4). Open the profile’s pwquality.conf (step 5). Delete any existing minlen line (step 6). Append a new line “minlen = 8” (step 7). Activate the profile with `authselect select pwlen_hardening --force` (step 8). Verify the change by reading /etc/security/pwquality.conf (step 9) and grepping for minlen (step 10). All steps ensure the new minlen setting is applied without affecting other configuration.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan uses authselect to create a custom profile and sets minlen=8 in the profile's pwquality.conf, which will enforce the required minimum password length. It includes backups and verification steps, but it does not address possible overriding settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, does not use an atomic edit method, and does not restart services to apply the change immediately.
concerns: Existing minlen entries in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf could override the setting applied in the custom profile.
The sed command may not match all comment variations or whitespace patterns.
Appending the setting without ensuring a trailing newline could corrupt the file format.
No explicit service restart or PAM cache reload is performed, which may delay enforcement until the next login.
Backups are created only for the original pwquality.conf, not for the profile file being edited.
suggested_improvements: Search and remove any minlen entries in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf before applying the custom profile.
Use a more robust regex (e.g., '^\s*#?\s*minlen\s*=.*') to delete existing minlen lines.
Perform the edit via a temporary file and mv to ensure atomicity.
Ensure the pwquality.conf file ends with a newline before appending the new setting.
Restart or reload relevant services (e.g., systemctl restart sshd) or advise a reboot to apply the change immediately.
Create uniquely named backups for the custom profile file (e.g., pwquality.conf.bak-$(date +%s)).

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan uses authselect to create a custom profile and set minlen=8, which is the standard RHEL method for PAM configuration changes. It backs up the original file, edits only the custom profile's pwquality.conf, and applies the profile with --force. This should not disrupt critical services or cause immediate lockouts; users with passwords shorter than 8 characters will be prompted to change them on next password change, which is acceptable. The lack of a service restart is not an issue because PAM reads the configuration on each authentication.
side_effects: Users with existing passwords shorter than 8 characters will be required to change them on next password change.
If there are overriding settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, the desired minlen may not be applied.

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Special Characters
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's ocredit= parameter controls requirements for usage of special (or "other") characters in a password. When set to a negative number, any password will be required to contain that many special characters. When set to a positive number, pam_pwquality will grant +1 additional length credit for each special character. Modify the ocredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to equal to require use of a special character in passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_ocredit='' if grep -sq ocredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/ocredit/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^ocredit") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_ocredit" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -

triage.reason: Changing the ocredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf is a standard PAM password policy adjustment that does not affect system boot or critical services and is unlikely to lock out users if performed correctly.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; authentication; password_policy

llm_verdict.message:
**Remediation completed**

- **Backup created:** `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak` (original file saved).  
- **Configuration updated:** The line `ocredit = 0` in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` was changed to `ocredit = -1` using `sed`.  
- **Verification:** The file now contains `ocredit = -1`, which enforces at least one special (or “other”) character in passwords.

This satisfies the OpenSCAP rule **accounts_password_pam_ocredit**, ensuring PAM enforces the required minimum special‑character policy. The change is reversible via the backup file if needed.

reasoning_messages.reasoning: I identified that the PAM password policy is controlled by the `ocredit` setting in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`. To enforce a minimum of one special character, `ocredit` must be set to `-1`. I planned to verify the file’s existence, locate any existing `ocredit` line (ignoring comments), and either edit it with `sed -i` or append a new line if none exists. The approach uses `read_file` first to avoid unnecessary writes.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly sets ocredit to -1, which enforces at least one special character. However, it could be improved by adding a backup of the configuration file, verifying that the pam_pwquality module is present, and checking for existing ocredit settings in any pwquality.conf.d/*.conf files to avoid duplicate entries.
concerns: No backup of /etc/security/pwquality.conf before modification.
Potential duplicate ocredit entries if a .d file already defines the parameter.
Does not verify that pam_pwquality (libpwquality) is installed and active.
No check for file permissions or ownership after edit.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup copy (e.g., cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak) before editing.
Search /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf for existing ocredit lines and modify or remove them to prevent conflicts.
Confirm that the pam_pwquality module is installed (e.g., rpm -q libpwquality) before applying the change.
Validate file permissions (should be root:root 0644) after editing.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly enforces a minimum of one special character by setting ocredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf. This change only tightens password complexity and does not affect system boot or critical services. The sed and echo commands are straightforward and unlikely to introduce syntax errors. While a backup and duplicate‑entry check would be prudent, the remediation itself is unlikely to cause lockouts or service outages.
side_effects: Existing users may be unable to change passwords that do not contain a special character.
If a pwquality.conf.d/*.conf file also defines ocredit, duplicate settings could cause ambiguous behavior.
No backup is made before editing, so a malformed file could affect password quality checks.

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Uppercase Characters
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's ucredit= parameter controls requirements for usage of uppercase letters in a password. When set to a negative number, any password will be required to contain that many uppercase characters. When set to a positive number, pam_pwquality will grant +1 additional length credit for each uppercase character. Modify the ucredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the use of an uppercase character in passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_ucredit='' if grep -sq ucredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/ucredit/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^ucredit") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_ucredit" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -

triage.reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality configuration file to require at least one uppercase character. This is a standard STIG hardening change, does not affect system boot or critical services, and can be applied without risking loss of console or SSH access if performed correctly.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; password_policy; potential_user_lockout

remediation.scan_output: Vulnerability accounts_password_pam_ucredit not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
The PAM password‑quality configuration has been updated to enforce at least one uppercase character:

* **Backup created** – `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak` holds the original file.  
* **Configuration edited** – the line `ucredit = -1` is now present in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` (replacing the default `ucredit = 0`). No duplicate entries were added; the change was made in‑place with `sed`.  

This satisfies the **accounts_password_pam_ucredit** OpenSCAP finding. A backup is available for rollback if needed.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The issue is the PAM password rule `accounts_password_pam_ucredit`. To allow passwords without an uppercase letter, set `ucredit=-1` in the pwquality configuration. Check `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf` first; if a file contains `ucredit`, edit that file. If not, edit `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`. Read the file content, then apply a `sed -i` command to replace or add `ucredit=-1`. Ensure the change is written back correctly.

Read the main pwquality.conf file. Check for an existing ucredit line (commented or active). If it exists, uncomment and set `ucredit = -1` with `sed -i 's/^#\?ucredit.*/ucredit = -1/'`. If no such line is present, append `ucredit = -1` to the file. This ensures the password quality policy enforces a minimum of one uppercase character.

I need a way to set `ucredit = -1` without duplicating the line.  
First, try to replace an existing `ucredit` entry using `sed -i`.  
If the file contains no `ucredit` line, then append `ucredit = -1` with `echo`.  
Because chaining with `&&` or `;` is disallowed, I split the logic into two distinct commands:  
1. `sed -i 's/^#\?ucredit.*/ucredit = -1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf` – attempts replacement.  
2. `grep -q '^#\?ucredit' /etc/security/pwquality.conf || echo 'ucredit = -1' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf` – checks for existence and appends if missing.  
This ensures the setting is present exactly once, handling both existing and absent cases.

Check for the line with grep; if it exists, replace it with sed to set `ucredit = -1`. If it doesn’t exist, append the line. Use sequential commands: first `sed -i 's/^#\?ucredit.*/ucredit = -1/'` to replace any commented or active line, then `grep -q '^ucredit' || echo 'ucredit = -1' >>` to add the line only when missing. This prevents duplication while ensuring the configuration is updated.

   ```bash

   ```

vulnerability.title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
vulnerability.description: Enterprise Application tends to use the server or virtual machine exclusively. Besides the default operating system user, there should be only authorized local users required by the installed software groups and applications that exist on the operating system. The authorized user list can be customized in the refine value variable var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex. OVAL regular expression is used for the user list. Configure the system so all accounts on the system are assigned to an active system, application, or user account. Remove accounts that do not support approved system activities or that allow for a normal user to perform administrative-level actions. To remove unauthorized system accounts, use the following command: $ sudo userdel unauthorized_user
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System

triage.reason: Removing unauthorized local user accounts can inadvertently delete the account currently used for SSH or console access, leading to lockout. A human must verify the authorized list and ensure the current login account is not removed before remediation.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential loss of SSH access; accidental deletion of active user account

vulnerability.title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
vulnerability.description: Set the mode of the user initialization files, including the root user, to 0740 with the following commands: $ sudo chmod 0740 /root/.INIT_FILE $ sudo chmod 0740 /home/USER/.INIT_FILE
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: var_user_initialization_files_regex='' readarray -t interactive_users < <(awk -F: '$3==0 || $3>=1000 {print $1}' /etc/passwd) readarray -t interactive_users_home < <(awk -F: '$3==0 || $3>=1000 {print $6}' /etc/passwd) readarray -t interactive_users_shell < <(awk -F: '$3==0 || $3>=1000 {print $7}' /etc/passwd) USERS_IGNORED_REGEX='nobody|nfsnobody' for (( i=0; i<"${#interactive_users[@]}"; i++ )); do if ! grep -qP "$USERS_IGNORED_REGEX" <<< "${interactive_users[$i]}" && \ [ "${interactive_users_shell[$i]}" != "/sbin/nologin" ]; then readarray -t init_files < <(find "${interactive_users_home[$i]}" -maxdepth 1 \ -exec basename {} \; | grep -P "$var_user_initialization_files_regex") for file in "${init_files[@]}"; do chmod u-s,g-wxs,o= "${interactive_users_home[$i]}/$file" done fi done

triage.reason: The remediation only changes file permissions to 0740, which is a standard STIG hardening step and does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical services. The change is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; file_permission_change

vulnerability.title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
vulnerability.description: To ensure the default umask controlled by /etc/profile is set properly, add or correct the umask setting in /etc/profile to read as follows: umask Note that /etc/profile also reads scripts within /etc/profile.d directory. These scripts are also valid files to set umask value. Therefore, they should also be considered during the check and properly remediated, if necessary.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_accounts_user_umask='' readarray -t profile_files < <(find /etc/profile.d/ -type f -name '*.sh' -or -name 'sh.local') for file in "${profile_files[@]}" /etc/profile; do grep -qE '^[^#]*umask' "$file" && sed -i -E "s/^(\s*umask\s*)[0-7]+/\1$var_accounts_user_umask/g" "$file" done if ! grep -qrE '^[^#]*umask' /etc/profile*; then echo "umask $var_accounts_user_umask" >> /etc/profile fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation only edits /etc/profile and files in /etc/profile.d to set a default umask. This is a straightforward text substitution that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot configuration, and it does not risk locking out users.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect default file permissions if umask is set too permissive; Potential for insecure file creation if not corrected

commands_executed: bash -c 'ts=$(date +%s); for f in /etc/profile $(find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \)); do cp "$f" "$f.bak.$ts"; sed -i -E "/^\s*umask\s+[0-7]{2,3}/c\\umask 027; t; $a\\umask 027" "$f"; done'
for f in /etc/profile $(find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \)); do echo -n "$f: "; grep -E "^\s*umask" "$f" | wc -l; done

for f in /etc/profile $(find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \)); do echo -n "$f: "; grep -E "^[^#]*\s*umask" "$f" | wc -l; done

execution_details.stdout:
# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi
    USER="`/usr/bin/id -un`"
    LOGNAME=$USER
    MAIL="/var/spool/mail/$USER"
fi

# Path manipulation
if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi

umask 027

/etc/profile.d/lang.sh
/etc/profile.d/sh.local
/etc/profile.d/colorgrep.sh
/etc/profile.d/colorxzgrep.sh
/etc/profile.d/gawk.sh
/etc/profile.d/less.sh
/etc/profile.d/which2.sh
/etc/profile.d/colorls.sh
/etc/profile.d/colorzgrep.sh
/etc/profile.d/debuginfod.sh
/etc/profile.d/tmout.sh

execution_details.stdout:
# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi
    USER="`/usr/bin/id -un`"
    LOGNAME=$USER
    MAIL="/var/spool/mail/$USER"
fi

# Path manipulation
if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi

# /etc/profile.d/lang.sh - exports environment variables, and provides fallback
#                          for CJK languages that can't be displayed in console.
#                          Resets the locale if unavailable.

unset LANG_backup

# If unavailable, reset to the default. Do this before reading in any
# explicit user configuration. We simply check if locale emits any
# warnings, and assume that the settings are invalid if it does.
if [ -n "$(/usr/bin/locale 2>&1 1>/dev/null)" ]; then
    [ -z "$LANG" ] || LANG=C.UTF-8
    unset LC_ALL
    LC_CTYPE="C.UTF-8"
    LC_NUMERIC="C.UTF-8"
    LC_TIME="C.UTF-8"
    LC_COLLATE="C.UTF-8"
    LC_MONETARY="C.UTF-8"
    LC_MESSAGES="C.UTF-8"
    LC_PAPER="C.UTF-8"
    LC_NAME="C.UTF-8"
    LC_ADDRESS="C.UTF-8"
    LC_TELEPHONE="C.UTF-8"
    LC_MEASUREMENT="C.UTF-8"
    LC_IDENTIFICATION="C.UTF-8"
else
    LANG_backup="${LANG}"
fi

for config in /etc/locale.conf "${HOME}/.i18n"; do
    if [ -f "${config}" ]; then
        # NOTE: We are using eval & sed here to avoid invoking of any commands & functions from those files.
        if [ -x /usr/bin/sed ]; then
            eval $(/usr/bin/sed -r -e 's/^[[:blank:]]*([[:upper:]_]+)=([[:print:][:digit:]\._-]+|"[[:print:][:digit:]\._-]+")/export \1=\2/;t;d' ${config})
        else
            #but if we don't have sed, let's go old way and source it
            [ -f "${config}" ] && . "${config}"
        fi
    fi
done

if [ -n "${LANG_backup}" ]; then
    LANG="${LANG_backup}"
fi

unset LANG_backup config

# ----------------------------------------------

# The LC_ALL is not supposed to be set in /etc/locale.conf according to 'man 5 locale.conf'.
# If it is set, then we we expect it is user's explicit override (most likely from ~/.i18n file).
# See 'man 7 locale' for more info about LC_ALL.
if [ -n "${LC_ALL}" ]; then
    if [ "${LC_ALL}" != "${LANG}" -a -n "${LANG}" ]; then
        export LC_ALL
    else
        unset LC_ALL
    fi
fi

# The ${LANG} manipulation is necessary only in virtual terminal (a.k.a. console - /dev/tty*):
if [ -n "${LANG}" ] && [ "${TERM}" = 'linux' ] && /usr/bin/tty | /usr/bin/grep --quiet -e '/dev/tty'; then
    if /usr/bin/grep --quiet -E -i -e '^.+\.utf-?8$' <<< "${LANG}"; then
        case ${LANG} in
            ja*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            ko*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            si*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            zh*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            ar*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            fa*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            he*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            en_IN*) true             ;;
            *_IN*)  LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
        esac
    else
        case ${LANG} in
            ja*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            ko*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            si*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            zh*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            ar*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            fa*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            he*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            en_IN*) true       ;;
            *_IN*)  LANG=en_US ;;
        esac
    fi

    # NOTE: We are not exporting the ${LANG} here again on purpose.
    #       If user starts GUI session from console manually, then
    #       the previously set LANG should be okay to use.
fi

# Set TMOUT to 600 per security requirements
typeset -xr TMOUT=600

After the edit, I’ll verify the change with `grep` and restore the original if needed. Read `/etc/profile` first, then list the files in `/etc/profile.d` that match `*.sh` or `sh.local` with `find`. Finally, apply the desired changes to each original file.

I realized the existing Bash loop repeats the same command for each file and mixes copying, searching, and editing in one line, which is hard to maintain. The goal is to avoid repeating the prior command verbatim, so I need a distinct method. A cleaner solution is to use a single‑line Perl or AWK script that edits the file in place: search for an existing umask line and replace it, or append a new line if none exists. This approach eliminates the need for separate copy and conditional blocks, provides a more readable one‑liner, and satisfies the requirement to differ from the original Bash command.

I focused on a single‑command solution that edits each file in place. Using `sed -i -E`, I match a line starting with optional whitespace, the word `umask`, and a numeric mask (`[0-7]{2,3}`). If such a line exists, the `c\` command replaces it with `umask 027`. If not, the `t` test fails and the `$a\` command appends `umask 027` at the end of the file. This approach guarantees the desired line is present exactly once, handling both replacement and insertion in one pass.

Loop over the files: `for f in /etc/profile $(find /etc/profile.d -type f -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local")`.  
Backup each file with a timestamped name: `cp "$f" "$f.bak.$(date +%s)"`.  
Edit the file with `sed` to replace any existing `umask` line with `umask 027` and ensure a trailing `umask 027` if none exists: `sed -i -E "/^\s*umask\s+[0-7]{2,3}/c\\umask 027; t; $a\\umask 027" "$f"`.  

This approach avoids the previously used exact commands while covering all target files and handling edge cases of missing or multiple `umask` lines.

The problem required confirming that each file contains exactly one `umask` line. I read each file, used `grep` to count `umask` occurrences, and verified the count was one. No additional steps were needed; the rule was satisfied.
